len(reviews_2016)

var_functions.query_db:2.text: Sure, it's cheap, but there isn't much to see. I think you'd have to have a big interest in the topic to find it exciting, and kids would be bored. I think it only lasted maybe 10-15 minutes. Our tour person was somewhat knowledgeable and seemed into it, but he didn't give us much time to read the information on each level. I had to take photos of the plaques to read later, and half of them need replacing, as they are so sun-bleached they're virtually unreadable (tour guide said they're being replaced soon and that the other half were already replaced). I really thought it needed to be higher up to give a good view. The Lewis and Clark State Historic Site just down the road was more interesting and free. If you live around here like I do and have nothing better to do, you might want to give it a go if the topic interests you, but if you're a tourist, this is not something you should waste your time on.
Very good service but a little pricey for the services your receive. Clean and sanitary too

My friend and I enjoyed a fantastic meal at Miles Table and I can't wait to return! Given that it was half-price-burger-day, I felt the need to give the falafel burger a shot. It was delicious! I'm not always a falafel fan, but this "burger" was awesome. The brioche bun was the perfect consistency and the portion of fries was plentiful. My friend enjoyed the cobb salad (though she requested balsamic dressing instead of buttermilk and she received buttermilk). The restaurant is quaint and has ample seating. While you order at the counter, our "server" (I believe her name was Sarah) was fantastic and made sure we had everything we needed. Overall, this is a great spot and I will be back soon...I may have to make it a weekly lunch staple!

Terrible service. I was charged twice for online order and they refused to refund me. Numerous times this has happened

import json
import re
from datetime import datetime
import pandas as pd

# Load the 2016 reviews from file
storage_key = 'var_functions.query_db:12'
result_file = globals()[storage_key]

with open(result_file, 'r') as f:
    reviews_2016 = json.load(f)

# Convert to DataFrame
df = pd.DataFrame(reviews_2016)

# Clean and parse dates - handle multiple formats
def parse_date(date_str):
    if not date_str:
        return None
    
    # Try different date formats
    formats = [
        '%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S',
        '%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S.%f',
        '%B %d, %Y at %I:%M %p',
        '%d %b %Y, %H:%M',
        '%d %b %Y, %I:%M %p',
        '%B %d, %Y at %H:%M %p'
    ]
    
    for fmt in formats:
        try:
            return datetime.strptime(str(date_str), fmt)
        except:
            continue
    
    # If all formats fail, try to extract year, month, day
    try:
        # Extract date parts using regex
        import re
        # Look for patterns like 2016-01-15 or 01/15/2016 or 15 Jan 2016
        date_patterns = [
            r'(\d{4})-(\d{1,2})-(\d{1,2})',
            r'(\d{1,2})/(\d{1,2})/(\d{4})',
            r'(\d{1,2})\s+(Jan|Feb|Mar|Apr|May|Jun|Jul|Aug|Sep|Oct|Nov|Dec)\s+(\d{4})',
            r'(Jan|Feb|Mar|Apr|May|Jun|Jul|Aug|Sep|Oct|Nov|Dec)\s+(\d{1,2}),?\s+(\d{4})'
        ]
        
        for pattern in date_patterns:
            match = re.search(pattern, str(date_str), re.IGNORECASE)
            if match:
                if pattern == date_patterns[0]:  # YYYY-MM-DD
                    year, month, day = match.groups()
                    return datetime(int(year), int(month), int(day))
                elif pattern == date_patterns[1]:  # MM/DD/YYYY
                    month, day, year = match.groups()
                    return datetime(int(year), int(month), int(day))
                elif pattern == date_patterns[2]:  # DD Mon YYYY
                    day, month_str, year = match.groups()
                    month_map = {'jan':1,'feb':2,'mar':3,'apr':4,'may':5,'jun':6,
                               'jul':7,'aug':8,'sep':9,'oct':10,'nov':11,'dec':12}
                    return datetime(int(year), month_map[month_str.lower()], int(day))
                elif pattern == date_patterns[3]:  # Mon DD YYYY
                    month_str, day, year = match.groups()
                    month_map = {'jan':1,'feb':2,'mar':3,'apr':4,'may':5,'jun':6,
                               'jul':7,'aug':8,'sep':9,'oct':10,'nov':11,'dec':12}
                    return datetime(int(year), month_map[month_str.lower()], int(day))
    except:
        pass
    
    return None

# Parse dates and filter for Jan 1 - Jun 30, 2016
df['date_parsed'] = df['date'].apply(parse_date)
start_date = datetime(2016, 1, 1)
end_date = datetime(2016, 6, 30, 23, 59, 59)

filtered_df = df[(df['date_parsed'] >= start_date) & (df['date_parsed'] <= end_date)]

# Calculate average rating and review count per business
business_stats = filtered_df.groupby('business_ref').agg({
    'rating': ['mean', 'count']
}).round(2)

business_stats.columns = ['avg_rating', 'review_count']
business_stats = business_stats.reset_index()

# Filter for businesses with at least 5 reviews
qualified_businesses = business_stats[business_stats['review_count'] >= 5]

# Sort by average rating descending
qualified_businesses = qualified_businesses.sort_values('avg_rating', ascending=False)

top_business = qualified_businesses.iloc[0] if not qualified_businesses.empty else None

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps({
    'total_reviews_2016': len(df),
    'filtered_reviews_in_range': len(filtered_df),
    'unique_businesses_in_range': len(filtered_df['business_ref'].unique()),
    'businesses_with_5plus_reviews': len(qualified_businesses),
    'top_business_ref': top_business['business_ref'] if top_business is not None else None,
    'top_business_avg_rating': float(top_business['avg_rating']) if top_business is not None else None,
    'top_business_review_count': int(top_business['review_count']) if top_business is not None else None
}))

import json
import re
from datetime import datetime
import pandas as pd

# Load the 2016 reviews from file
storage_key = 'var_functions.query_db:12'
result_file = globals()[storage_key]

with open(result_file, 'r') as f:
    reviews_2016 = json.load(f)

# Convert to DataFrame and ensure rating is numeric
df = pd.DataFrame(reviews_2016)
df['rating'] = pd.to_numeric(df['rating'], errors='coerce')

# Clean and parse dates - handle multiple formats
def parse_date(date_str):
    if not date_str or pd.isna(date_str):
        return None
    
    # Try different date formats
    formats = [
        '%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S',
        '%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S.%f',
        '%B %d, %Y at %I:%M %p',
        '%d %b %Y, %H:%M',
        '%d %b %Y, %I:%M %p',
        '%B %d, %Y at %H:%M %p'
    ]
    
    for fmt in formats:
        try:
            return datetime.strptime(str(date_str), fmt)
        except:
            continue
    
    # If all formats fail, try to extract date parts using regex
    try:
        # Look for patterns like 2016-01-15 or 01/15/2016 or 15 Jan 2016
        date_patterns = [
            r'(\d{4})-(\d{1,2})-(\d{1,2})',
            r'(\d{1,2})/(\d{1,2})/(\d{4})',
            r'(\d{1,2})\s+(Jan|Feb|Mar|Apr|May|Jun|Jul|Aug|Sep|Oct|Nov|Dec)\s+(\d{4})',
            r'(Jan|Feb|Mar|Apr|May|Jun|Jul|Aug|Sep|Oct|Nov|Dec)\s+(\d{1,2}),?\s+(\d{4})'
        ]
        
        for pattern in date_patterns:
            match = re.search(pattern, str(date_str), re.IGNORECASE)
            if match:
                if pattern == date_patterns[0]:  # YYYY-MM-DD
                    year, month, day = match.groups()
                    return datetime(int(year), int(month), int(day))
                elif pattern == date_patterns[1]:  # MM/DD/YYYY
                    month, day, year = match.groups()
                    return datetime(int(year), int(month), int(day))
                elif pattern == date_patterns[2]:  # DD Mon YYYY
                    day, month_str, year = match.groups()
                    month_map = {'jan':1,'feb':2,'mar':3,'apr':4,'may':5,'jun':6,
                               'jul':7,'aug':8,'sep':9,'oct':10,'nov':11,'dec':12}
                    return datetime(int(year), month_map[month_str.lower()], int(day))
                elif pattern == date_patterns[3]:  # Mon DD YYYY
                    month_str, day, year = match.groups()
                    month_map = {'jan':1,'feb':2,'mar':3,'apr':4,'may':5,'jun':6,
                               'jul':7,'aug':8,'sep':9,'oct':10,'nov':11,'dec':12}
                    return datetime(int(year), month_map[month_str.lower()], int(day))
    except:
        pass
    
    return None

# Parse dates and filter for Jan 1 - Jun 30, 2016
df['date_parsed'] = df['date'].apply(parse_date)
start_date = datetime(2016, 1, 1)
end_date = datetime(2016, 6, 30, 23, 59, 59)

filtered_df = df[(df['date_parsed'] >= start_date) & (df['date_parsed'] <= end_date)].copy()

# Calculate average rating and review count per business
business_stats = filtered_df.groupby('business_ref').agg({
    'rating': ['mean', 'count']
}).reset_index()

business_stats.columns = ['business_ref', 'avg_rating', 'review_count']
business_stats['avg_rating'] = business_stats['avg_rating'].round(2)

# Filter for businesses with at least 5 reviews
qualified_businesses = business_stats[business_stats['review_count'] >= 5]

# Sort by average rating descending
qualified_businesses = qualified_businesses.sort_values('avg_rating', ascending=False)

top_business = qualified_businesses.iloc[0] if not qualified_businesses.empty else None

result = {
    'total_reviews_2016': len(df),
    'filtered_reviews_in_range': len(filtered_df),
    'unique_businesses_in_range': len(filtered_df['business_ref'].unique()) if not filtered_df.empty else 0,
    'businesses_with_5plus_reviews': len(qualified_businesses),
    'top_business_ref': top_business['business_ref'] if top_business is not None else None,
    'top_business_avg_rating': float(top_business['avg_rating']) if top_business is not None else None,
    'top_business_review_count': int(top_business['review_count']) if top_business is not None else None
}

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(result))

var_functions.query_db:2.text: Sure, it's cheap, but there isn't much to see. I think you'd have to have a big interest in the topic to find it exciting, and kids would be bored. I think it only lasted maybe 10-15 minutes. Our tour person was somewhat knowledgeable and seemed into it, but he didn't give us much time to read the information on each level. I had to take photos of the plaques to read later, and half of them need replacing, as they are so sun-bleached they're virtually unreadable (tour guide said they're being replaced soon and that the other half were already replaced). I really thought it needed to be higher up to give a good view. The Lewis and Clark State Historic Site just down the road was more interesting and free. If you live around here like I do and have nothing better to do, you might want to give it a go if the topic interests you, but if you're a tourist, this is not something you should waste your time on.

My friend and I enjoyed a fantastic meal at Miles Table and I can't wait to return! Given that it was half-price-burger-day, I felt the need to give the falafel burger a shot. It was delicious! I'm not always a falafel fan, but this "burger" was awesome. The brioche bun was the perfect consistency and the portion of fries was plentiful. My friend enjoyed the cobb salad (though she requested balsamic dressing instead of buttermilk and she received buttermilk). The restaurant is quaint and has ample seating. While you order at the counter, our "server" (I believe her name was Sarah) was fantastic and made sure we had everything we needed. Overall, this is a great spot and I will be back soon...I may have to make it a weekly lunch staple!
